items.prompt_md: In the one-pass hash-map solution, what do you store as the map's **key**?
options: The value at the current position
items.answer: The value at the current position
items.explanation_md: You store each value so a later element can ask "has my complement been seen?" The index is the *value* you keep alongside it, for the answer.

items.prompt_md: You are given an **unsorted** array and must decide whether any two elements add up to a target `k`, returning their positions. Which approach fits with no sorting and a single pass?
options: Sort the array, then walk two pointers inward
Slide a window and shrink it when the sum is too large
Keep a hash map of values seen so far and look up each complement
Try every pair with two nested loops
items.answer: Keep a hash map of values seen so far and look up each complement
items.explanation_md: Unsorted + "have I already seen the partner I need?" + O(n) target is the fingerprint for a hash-map complement lookup. Sorting would destroy the original indices the question asks for.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the one-pass hash-map solution to Two Sum?
items.explanation_md: One scan of the array, and each map insert/lookup is expected O(1), so the whole pass is O(n) time and O(n) extra space.
